at_a_glance: Iran talks falter as Strait of Hormuz tensions raise risk to global fuel supplies.
EU airlines warn of jet fuel shortages if the waterway remains closed; tankers told not to pay Iranian tolls.
Ukraine and Russia trade blame for hundreds of ceasefire violations amid intensified strikes.
At least 30 feared dead in a crush at a Haitian tourist site, with rescue efforts ongoing.
British Steel ‘needs nationalising by the summer’, piling pressure on ministers over industrial strategy.
Asha Bhosle, the voice of generations of Bollywood cinema, dies aged 92.

stories.title: After Iran talks falter, the big question is what happens next?
stories.summary: Fragile diplomacy with Iran has stumbled, amplifying fears over the Strait of Hormuz and the knock‑on effects on global fuel supplies. Europe’s airlines warn of jet fuel shortages if the choke point remains shut, while shippers are urged not to pay Iranian tolls.
body: The latest effort to ease tensions with Tehran has run aground, leaving a dangerous vacuum around the world’s most vital oil and fuel corridor. With the diplomatic track stalling, attention has turned to the Strait of Hormuz, where any prolonged disruption would reverberate through energy and transport markets.

Industry bodies in Europe warn that a sustained closure could trigger jet fuel shortages and force carriers to ration or reroute operations. Shipping groups, meanwhile, are being urged not to pay any transit tolls demanded by Iran, a stance that raises complex legal, sanctions and insurance questions for operators assessing risk.

Energy traders are already gaming out contingencies, from longer voyages around Africa to accelerated stockpiling, all of which would increase costs and travel times. Even partial interruptions can ripple quickly into higher fares, pricier freight and renewed pressure on inflation forecasts.

Political calls for restraint are growing, with Starmer urging Washington and Tehran to find a way through before miscalculation hardens into crisis. The next inflection points to watch include back‑channel talks, potential convoy protections and whether any narrow, temporary reopening can stabilise markets long enough for diplomacy to resume.

stories.title: Ukraine and Russia accuse each other of hundreds of ceasefire violations
stories.summary: Kyiv and Moscow traded allegations of large‑scale truce breaches amid intensified shelling and drone strikes. The claims underscore how fragile any lull remains, with civilians again bearing the brunt.
body: Both sides reported surges in violations along multiple sectors of the front, each blaming the other for escalating attacks. The tempo of strikes has sharpened the battle of narratives, with information and military campaigns moving in lockstep.

Civilian areas have faced renewed disruption, from power cuts to damage to homes and infrastructure, as emergency services race to stabilise hard‑hit districts. Independent verification of casualty figures remains difficult amid restricted access and ongoing hostilities.

Even limited ceasefire understandings are proving brittle, vulnerable to local tactical moves and shifting battlefield priorities. The absence of durable monitoring mechanisms complicates efforts to assign responsibility and to halt spirals of retaliation.

International partners are urging restraint while funnelling humanitarian aid and air‑defence support, wary that a further slide could derail wider diplomatic efforts. For now, the frontline remains fluid, with the risk that local flare‑ups metastasise into broader confrontations.

stories.title: British Steel ‘needs nationalising by the summer’ as policy tensions sharpen
stories.summary: A stark warning that British Steel may require nationalisation within months has thrust industrial policy back to the top of the agenda. It comes as ministers are said to be relaxed about Chinese car imports, highlighting a split‑screen approach to competitiveness and consumer prices.
body: Pressure is intensifying on the government to decide whether, and how, to intervene in Britain’s steelmaking future after warnings that British Steel could need to be brought into public ownership by the summer. The prospect underscores fragile balance sheets and the capital demands of the transition to lower‑carbon production.

At the same time, analysis by Faisal Islam suggests ministers are relatively sanguine about inflows of competitively priced Chinese electric vehicles, reflecting a calculus that cheaper consumer goods help tame inflation even as domestic manufacturers seek protection and investment.

Bridging those tensions will require choices on state aid, carbon costs and border measures, alongside clarity on timelines for green steel. Without it, the sector risks drift, deterring private capital and imperilling skilled jobs across critical industrial hubs.

Officials are weighing options from bridge financing and conditional support to a special administration regime, while unions press for job guarantees and a credible decarbonisation plan. Markets, suppliers and customers are watching closely for signals that policy will match rhetoric with delivery.

stories.title: Asha Bhosle: The sound of Bollywood dies aged 92
stories.summary: Asha Bhosle, whose voice defined generations of Hindi cinema, has died aged 92. Her versatility and prolific output made her a defining figure of South Asian popular culture.
body: Across decades of playback singing, Bhosle brought charisma, range and emotional colour to thousands of film songs, helping to shape the musical identity of Bollywood for audiences in India and far beyond. Her recordings became part of the cultural fabric, spanning romance, drama and dance classics.

Colleagues and admirers from film and music paid tribute to an artist whose catalogue bridged eras and styles. To many, she embodied the glamour and storytelling of the industry at its most expansive, her voice inseparable from the stars who mimed to it on screen.

For fans at home and across the diaspora, her songs trace personal histories as much as cinematic ones, soundtracking weddings, festivals and family memory. Broadcasters and platforms are set to revisit her work, underscoring the breadth of an unmatched repertoire.

Her legacy is secure: a body of work that travelled across languages and generations, and a standard that continues to influence singers today. In mourning her, the industry also celebrates the voice that helped define its golden age.
